data.title: Silicon Motion: Execution Outpaces Expectations As MonTitan And Mobile Drive Tactical Upside
data.description:
Back in June, I laid out a few major reasons I was bullish on Silicon Motion Technology Corporation (NASDAQ:SIMO), and one, sector leadership in a growing market, was the main standout. None of that’s changed, and in fact, a couple of those levers look stronger. For example, PCIe 5.0 controllers now make up about 10% of the company’s client SSD revenue, well ahead of anything I could have expected.

Additionally, MonTitan has moved from being a promising technology to actively ramping with Tier 1s, and eMMC/UFS got a solid shot in the arm from China OEM restocking. We can also mix in firmer NAND pricing and leaner inventories, and the backdrop is better than I thought it would be by this point in the year.

Consequently, I’m keeping my call at a Buy because the risk/reward tilts positive here. The execution we’ve just seen gives me more confidence in the durability of this rebound, even though this is still a cyclical name that will ebb and flow with NAND pricing and enterprise ramps.

A Snapshot of Q2 2025’s Numbers

With Q2 2025’s numbers, the rebound showed up earlier than I penciled in. Revenue hit $198.7 million, up 19% from Q1. However, it was a 6% drop from the same period in the previous year. Gross margin moved up to nearly 2 percentage points YoY to 47.7%, and the company’s non‑GAAP operating margin climbed to 12.8% from 8.9% in Q1. That’s more operating leverage than I expected this soon, and it tells me management is actively improving the mix and controlling costs.

So, What’s Driving The Bullishness?

When I wrapped up my June piece, I made the case that the real proof of Silicon Motion’s recovery would come down to execution in the second half, demand recovery, and signs that the product pipeline could translate into actual dollars. Q2 2025 gave me some early answers, and there are a few levers here that I think investors should pay close attention to.

Client SSD: PCIe Gen5 ramp is real

The SM2508 controller, which is Silicon Motion’s flagship PCIe 5.0 product, now accounts for roughly 10% of client SSD revenue. That’s not just a technical milestone; it’s a signal that OEM adoption is happening ahead of schedule. I had penciled in a meaningful Gen5 mix closer to Q4 or even early 2026, but this early traction suggests ASP uplift and design win momentum are already feeding through. I think the outlook here is incredibly positive, especially with Gen5 SSDs becoming increasingly standard in high‑end laptops and gaming rigs, so this mix shift should continue to support SIMO’s margin expansion.

Enterprise SSD: MonTitan is finally ramping

This one’s been a slow burn. MonTitan always looked promising on paper (high‑performance, customizable, built for hyperscale), but in my view, the real question for investors was whether it would translate into the sort of revenue that rerates multiples. If we dig into the commentary from the Q2 earnings call, we can see that the quarter confirmed that multiple Tier‑1 customers are now actively ramping deployments. That’s a big deal because while it doesn’t make SIMO an enterprise heavyweight overnight, it validates the company’s strategy and opens the door to more predictable growth in FY26. I’ll be watching closely to see how this scales, especially in the context of BlueField DPU boot drive wins later this year.

eMMC/UFS: China handset cycle offers near‑term lift

I think this segment was the most surprising in terms of the upside. I didn’t focus too much on how it would factor into the bigger picture earlier, even though it’s clear that it can be a major contributor. Here, growth was a 40–45% sequential increase in eMMC/UFS revenue, albeit a lot of that was driven by higher restocking demand from Chinese smartphone OEMs. I’m not quite sure it will be a sustained growth engine, but it can definitely contribute to the topline and act as a useful buffer against SSD volatility.

The main takeaway for me is that it shows that SIMO’s controller portfolio is still relevant in midrange and entry‑level mobile platforms, and that is a big benefit because they tend to be less cyclical than flagship devices.

Automotive: Early traction, long runway

Automotive is still a small piece of the pie, but the signs are there that it will start scaling soon, so I think it’s worth flagging. Management says that the segment can start contributing as much as 10% of the company’s revenue over the next year or so, and that is backed up by a few key developments. First, SIMO’s ASPICE Level 3‑certified SSD controllers are now shipping into EV platforms, and the company is engaged with multiple automotive Tier 1s like Toyota, Honda, and BYD. These are big names that understand the market’s direction, especially in China.

Also, automotive storage demand is growing (your car’s infotainment, ADAS, and over‑the‑air updates all require reliable flash), and SIMO is positioning itself as a credible supplier. Right now, I’d file automotive as a non-material revenue driver, but given that it’s a long‑term optionality play, we can consider it by Q3 or Q4, when we have more solid numbers to work with in terms of the various products and pricing across the segment. That way, we can see the potential revenue added and how it could become more meaningful by 2026–2027, whether it can really hit that 10% number that management is so buoyed by.

Valuation & Outlook

Let’s be clear: I don’t think that SIMO is a cheap stock on an absolute basis, but it’s not at the level it should be relative to its trajectory. The market’s still treating it like a cyclical laggard at the same time that the fundamentals are quietly shifting toward secular growth.

What the multiples are telling me:

  • P/E compression is coming: The consensus EPS growth for FY26 is north of 40%. If that happens, SIMO’s forward P/E drops into the mid-teens, and you’re looking at a growth story trading at value multiples.

  • EV/EBITDA is elevated, but justified: The 16.6x forward multiple looks a bit rich until you factor in the Gen5 mix shift and MonTitan ramp-up over the next few quarters. They’re structural margin drivers, and going off the company’s projected revenue growth, I’m expecting EBITDA margin expansion of 300–400 bps over the next 18 months.

  • Dividend yield is quietly attractive: At 2.55%, SIMO’s yield compares very favorably to its sector multiples across the board. In my view, the yield is a signal of balance sheet strength and capital discipline, especially because the company has zero debt and $208M in cash, so there’s room to flex.

Also, when we compare SIMO to peers like DIOD (53x FWD P/E) or POWI (89x FWD P/E), SIMO’s 29x forward P/E looks reasonable, especially given its diversified product mix and improving margin profile. Of course, SIMO’s current revenue growth is more modest than either of those two, and it looks nearly inconsequential compared to SYNA, but its controller business is starting to show similar operating leverage, so the revenue upside is obvious.

I believe that if SIMO executes on Gen5 and MonTitan, we might see the stock well on its way to $95–100 by mid‑2026, especially if the Street starts to re-rate the enterprise SSD narrative.

Risks

Every thesis has its risks, and I’m not blind to the ones that exist for SIMO. I just believe they’re manageable or unlikely to have much of an effect, and that’s why I’m still bullish.

1. Client SSD demand softens post-Q4. The Gen5 ramp is real, but it’s also cyclical. If OEMs front-load purchases ahead of holiday builds, we could see a volume dip in Q1. To be clear, that’s not going to break my thesis, but it could pressure margins short-term. However, the ASP uplift from Gen5 should offset volume softness. Plus, SIMO’s exposure to gaming and other consumer laptops gives it a buffer most controller peers don’t have.

2. MonTitan fails to scale beyond initial deployments. Enterprise SSD is an exciting, big-play arena for SIMO, so if MonTitan stalls or we see hyperscaler adoption plateau, then the margin expansion story can lose steam. Fortunately, I don’t think this is likely to happen, given that early traction is coming from multiple Tier 1s, not just one anchor customer. And with BlueField boot drive wins in the pipeline, SIMO has a second wedge into enterprise flash. I’m watching the quarterly revenue mix closely.

3. Regulatory or geopolitical shocks. SIMO has a fair bit of exposure to China, both in its supply chain and the customer base, so any escalation in US-China trade tensions could disrupt some parts of its operations. On that front, though, the company is largely diversified across Korea, Taiwan, and the US, so it shouldn’t be a problem. Plus, the controller business is less exposed to export controls than full-stack NAND or DRAM players.

Final Thoughts

Silicon Motion is executing, plain and simple. The Gen5 ramp is on schedule, MonTitan is finally monetizing, mobile gave us tactical upside, and automotive is quietly gaining traction. We can also see that margins are expanding, guidance is firm, and the balance sheet is clean. The market’s still pricing this like a cyclical controller vendor, but the fundamentals are shifting. As such, I’m reiterating my Buy.

data.title: Lazard International Equity Select Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

  • International equity markets rose significantly during the second quarter, adding to the YTD lead vs. the US
  • International corporate profit growth remained resilient amid a volatile geopolitical environment
  • International currencies remained strong as the US Dollar experienced its worst first half of the year since 1973
  • The risk-on environment saw Small Caps outperform Large, and Growth outperform Value, with rates relatively stable
  • Quality continues to lag the market by historic amounts, a function of higher exposure to US revenue

International equities continued their strong run in 2025. The MSCI ACW ex-US and MSCI EAFE Indexes rose a strong 12% in the quarter, adding to their robust start to the year. Year to date, the MSCI ACW ex-US and MSCI EAFE Indexes have risen 18% and 19% respectively. International markets have outperformed the US by over 12% year to date. Strong international currencies have bolstered international outperformance. Prospects for fiscal stimulus and defense spending in Europe, coupled with geopolitical and budgetary uncertainty in the US, catalyzed a strong move in major international currencies versus the dollar with the Euro and Yen up 13% and 8% respectively year to date.

The quarter began with a significant increase in global tariffs and ended with a major military escalation in the Middle East. Very few investors would have anticipated a double-digit return for most global equity markets, but that’s what happened. Within a few days of the April 2 US tariff announcement, the S&P 500 Index and the MSCI EAFE Index fell roughly 14% and 11% respectively. At that point, the US announced a pause to the tariff implementation and markets quickly recovered. Earnings reporting season then ensued and international corporate profits demonstrated continued resilience, pushing markets higher. Earnings ultimately drive stock prices and earnings outside the US are rising.

Quality as a style continues to underperform not only the broader market but also both Growth and Value. Quality companies have seen their valuations derate significantly over the past few quarters. In fact, the degree of derating over the past year for developed markets has been the largest in the history of the MSCI indexes, exceeding the Global Financial Crisis in 2008/09.

Portfolio Review

During the second quarter, the Lazard International Equity Select Portfolio rose 11.3% (net of fees), lagging the MSCI ACW ex-US Index which rose 12.0%. The investment philosophy of the Lazard International Equity Platform is one of Relative Value where we contrast a company’s level and trajectory of financial productivity with its valuation. We believe that businesses with higher and more sustainable levels of financial productivity are deserving of higher valuations. We seek to buy these higher quality companies at relatively inexpensive valuations compared to the level of returns they generate.

When looking at the two fundamental factors we evaluate for each company, financial productivity and valuation, the Lazard International Equity Select Strategy is more biased toward quality and higher financial productivity. The premium to financial productivity compared to the benchmark is substantial and has posed a very significant headwind to relative performance during much of the post-COVID era as Quality significantly underperformed.

Looking back over nearly a quarter century, on a rolling 12-month basis, Quality has never lagged the market by more. Over this long period, the probability of Quality underperforming by this magnitude is over a 3 standard deviation event, or much less than a 1.0% chance. Over the long term, and inclusive of the past year, Quality has outperformed the market, on average, by over 2%, and we expect this to continue as we look forward.

Why is Quality underperforming? Over the past year, the valuation of the highest quintile of ROE within the MSCI ACW ex-US Index has fallen by nearly 20% relative to the benchmark, an amount second to the only comparative period which was the Global Financial Crisis.

We think valuations have derated due to two main factors: Higher inflation / interest rates, and US revenue exposure. Quality companies tend to be longer duration equities and can be more sensitive to the trajectory of longer-term rates. We think much of the move higher in rates is in the past and with it so too is this driver of the derating of Quality. In addition to higher rates / inflation as a driver of the derating, Quality companies tend to have higher revenue exposure to the US than the market. Because our portfolio is more skewed towards Quality, this effect also negatively impacted our performance. From a year-to-date attribution standpoint, companies in our portfolio with higher exposure to the US than the benchmark underperformed by roughly 482 basis points (bps) whereas those with lower exposure outperformed by 361 bps. With forward earnings growth expectations for US companies now converging with International earnings growth expectations, we also believe this driver of the derating is also behind us.

The Portfolio continues to be positioned in high quality compounders with a significant 30%+ premium in financial productivity.

We think many of the drivers of the Quality derating and resulting underperformance are behind us and are very excited about an improvement for Quality performance going forward. In the past quarter century, every time Quality underperformed the market by 5% or more, it outperformed in the subsequent period. We believe companies that can organically grow earnings while maintaining margins and profitability will be leaders going forward and therefore, we expect history to repeat.

Positives

Stock specific contributors to performance in the industrials sector included:

  • Diploma (1.2% weighting in the Portfolio) is domiciled in the UK and is an industrial distributor of small-ticket, mission critical operating expense items for broad categories of life sciences, seals, and controls. By utilizing a decentralized, autonomous business model, the company has been able to generate superior financial returns with a mid-teens ROIC. Historically, M&A has proven to be an excellent use of Diploma’s capital. The M&A strategy generally consists of small bolt-on deals, with limited bidding competition where the idea comes from the individual business units. Diploma is rarely the highest bidder but can assure the founder/seller that the business will be invested for growth, management usually stays on board and the business is left to run autonomously in the Diploma decentralized structure. Shares rose after the company reported first half results. Organic growth and EBITDA came in ahead of consensus expectations by 3% and 5% respectively and full year guidance was raised. These results are supportive of our investment thesis, and we added to the Portfolio’s position. We believe, over the mid-term, Diploma could grow 6-7%+ organically, plus an additional 5-10% via M&A (7% in H1), earn margins greater than 20% and a ROIC of ~15% with good resiliency in downturns.
  • MTU Aero Engines (2.1% weighting) is a German aerospace engine supplier. Outside of engine manufacturing, the company’s other main source of revenue comes from repairing other types of engines at its maintenance, repair, and overhaul (MRO) shops. Spare parts are extremely high margin and engine maintenance is a regulated activity that must be performed after a certain number of engine cycles, or take-offs and landings. We believe MTU offers the purest exposure to the aftermarket space, directly through its OEM division (engines and spare parts) and secondarily through its MRO segment. Shares rose after the company reported first quarter results. Sales and earnings came in ahead of consensus expectations, driven by strong aftermarket performance, and free cash flow also came in ahead of consensus expectations. These results are supportive of our investment thesis, and we maintained the Portfolio’s position.

Lower-than-index exposure to the underperforming energy sector positively contributed to relative returns.

Negatives

Companies with relatively higher US revenue exposure were among the largest detractors from performance.

  • Shares of Ireland-based global Insurance broker Aon (2.1% weighting) declined after the company reported slightly weaker-than-expected first quarter results. While the operational performance was largely as expected, investors sold off the shares as foreign exchange headwinds impacted reported sales leading to a slightly lower-than-expected earnings per share in the quarter. Favorably, management reiterated full-year guidance for mid-single-digit or greater organic growth and expectation for ~80 to 90 basis points (bps) of year-over-year (Y/Y) operating margin expansion towards ~32.3-32.4% (up from ~31.5% in 2024). We believe the share price reaction was overdone and the long-term earnings power of the company remains intact. As a broker and an asset-light company in a fairly consolidated industry, Aon generates strong returns on capital which we believe will be sustained due to strong pricing power and cash flow generation. We believe the earnings power, rather than quarter to quarter changes in top line growth rates, will drive the shares long term. We believe the stock remains inexpensive trading on a ~17.5x 2026E PE for a firm that we believe will deliver mid-to-high single-digit top-line growth and low-double-digit adjusted EPS growth over the medium-term in a secularly growing market. We maintained the Portfolio’s position.
  • Domiciled in Switzerland, Chubb (0.9% weighting) is one of the world’s most prominent and reputable insurance companies. Shares declined after the company reported mixed first quarter results where the continuation of better-than-expected strong underwriting performance across all segments was offset by the continuation of lighter-than-expected Net Premiums Written (NPW) and Net Premiums Earned (NPE). We maintained the Portfolio’s position and continue to monitor the situation.
  • Based in Hong Kong, Techtronic (1.2% weighting) is a leading global designer, manufacturer, and marketer of home improvement products. The majority of Techtronic’s revenue is derived from its power tools business which operates under the brands Milwaukee (professional power tools) and Ryobi (consumer power tools). Shares underperformed on tariff-related headwinds. We added to the Portfolio’s position, taking advantage of share price weakness.

Outlook

  • We believe heightened geopolitical uncertainty is likely to persist, and may result in slower investment and consumption
  • Central bank options could be limited by increasing fiscal deficits
  • We believe the drivers of US outperformance over the past several years may be diminishing Large International valuation discount and similar earnings growth vs. US suggest more upside following strong first half returns
  • In our view, historic quality underperformance presents a significant opportunity for outperformance going forward
  • Critical shift in investor sentiment should continue to benefit international markets and relative value investing

Quality as a style has outperformed the market over time. Yet, over the past year, Quality has underperformed significantly. This was due to the derating of Quality valuations resulting from higher rates as well as more exposure to US revenue. We think these negative effects on Quality are likely in the past and the historic pullback in valuations could offer a compelling opportunity to invest in the long-term compounding of the earnings of companies with high financial productivity at relatively attractive valuations.

We believe companies with stronger pricing power are more equipped for the coming environment. They can more easily pass along higher costs to their customers without seeing demand deteriorate and will be more likely to maintain their margins. Companies with the widest moats, the highest financial productivity and the opportunity to reinvest for growth should resume leadership.

The topic of tariffs continued to be top of mind in the second quarter. The US’s pause on tariff implementations breathed a sigh of relief into global equity markets and perhaps pulled in a little demand as well. Company managements continue to determine what tariffs mean for their businesses – and if they will even be implemented at all. From sales demand, to pricing, to margins – this new policy affects many facets of investment planning and decision making. In the near term, we believe management teams will need to wait to see how the situation sorts out. The initial inventory buildup ahead of potential tariff implementation may result in sales and profits being negatively impacted. We continue to speak with all of our companies trying to learn how these new policies will impact their businesses. Stock selection will be critical to sorting out winners and losers.

We expect central bank policy to continue to be top of mind. As of the end of the second quarter, the fixed income market is expecting the Federal Reserve and European Central bank to cut rates 50 basis points(bps) and 25 bps respectively by the end of 2025. Further accommodation is expected in 2026. With unemployment still reasonably low and the prospect for increasing fiscal deficits, the pace and aggressiveness of accommodation relative to expectations will likely be an area of focus for investors.

Since the Global Financial Crisis, global equity market returns have been led by the US. Initially that had much to do with how quickly US companies recovered from the GFC, but more recently the outperformance of US equities has been largely attributable to three key factors: Dollar strength, the concentration and outsized impact of the Magnificent 7, and these two factors combined to help drive the valuation of the US market to extraordinary levels. All three of these factors are reversing or diminishing and we think International equities can continue to outperform although it is unlikely to the degree we have seen in the first half of 2025.

With the valuation discount of International equities still near all-time wide levels, we think International equities can continue to outperform.

data.title: Albemarle Corporation Is Bullish (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
In this article, I will outline the bullish thesis for Albemarle Corporation (NYSE:ALB). Albemarle is a Specialty Chemicals company that operates in the Materials sector of the economy. They are known as a lithium producer. The bullish thesis for ALB consists of the need for lithium batteries, which I will discuss further, and the bullish price action and momentum that I will further outline.

I will start with the commodity that ALB is specifically known for, which is lithium. ALB produces various lithium compounds which are used in lithium batteries. These lithium batteries are used in consumer electronics, electric vehicles, solar panels, power grids, consumer appliances, etc. ALB also makes specialty chemicals for agriculture, aerospace, pharmaceutical, conventional energy, and construction industries.

The demand for lithium batteries is high, but there has recently been an oversupply of lithium on the market. This has caused record low prices for lithium, therefore hurting the stock price of ALB. However, that supply imbalance is showing signs of change.

Due to the record-low prices of lithium, producers such as ALB cut production, which has reduced the supply on the market. This has led to some higher prices. Now there is an expectation that 2026 and beyond will see increased lithium demand, according to an industry expert.

From a supply and demand perspective, ALB seems bullish. They produce a product that is expected to see higher prices and higher demand for the next several years. A so-called “mega trend.” This should lead to improved earnings in 2026 and increased revenues in 2026, according to analysts. ALB management even stated on the most recent earnings call that ALB will be free cash flow positive in 2025 due to its recent cost cutting as long as lithium prices remain where they are now.

Now I will provide a bullish thesis for ALB based on the technical analysis elements that I use whenever I make an investment decision. That is price action, momentum, volume, and relative strength.

Chart 1: ALB Weekly with 30-Week EMA, Momentum, Volume, and Relative Strength

Chart 1 shows the weekly price action of ALB for the past six years along with its 30-week exponential moving average (EMA). I am a trend-following trader, and I use price action, filtered by the 30-week EMA, to determine which way the trend is moving. I consider a stock that is trading above its upward-sloping 30-week EMA to be bullish. This is a stock that is trending higher.

Looking at Chart 1, you can see that, as a general rule, when ALB was trading above its 30-week EMA, that was good for investors who held ALB in their accounts. The best example was from mid-2020 until the end of 2022. ALB was generally above its 30-week EMA the whole time, and investors were rewarded with staying invested in ALB.

Conversely, when ALB started to trade below its 30-week EMA, that was the sign to sell ALB and look for other opportunities. You can see that for most of 2023 until June 2025, ALB traded below its downward-sloping 30-week EMA. That was the sign to not be long ALB. Using this simple filter of the 30-week EMA would have allowed you to participate in the large bullish move higher and not suffer the long duration and brutal selloff.

Right now, ALB is showing signs of trending higher. It is above its 30-week EMA and that EMA is sloping higher. This looks to be similar to early 2020. The trend of ALB is bullish.

Momentum is the next technical element I consider when buying a stock. Momentum is measured using the Percentage Price Oscillator (PPO). The PPO indicator is easy to use. When the black PPO line is above the red signal line, that is an indication of bullish short-term momentum. That is what we have now. When the black PPO line crosses below the red signal line that shows bearish short-term momentum.

More importantly, the PPO indicator shows long-term momentum as well. When the black PPO line has a reading that is greater than zero, that shows bullish long-term momentum. Conversely, when the black PPO lines read less than zero, that shows bearish long-term momentum. Right now, the black PPO line reads 2.231, which shows long-term bullish momentum.

Looking again at Chart 1, you can see that the black PPO line stays above or below zero for months or years at a time. This is why I say that indicates long-term momentum. You can also see that when ALB was trading above its 30-week EMA and the black PPO line was above zero, good things happened to ALB shareholders. In mid-2020, the black PPO line started to read greater than zero. Then the stock took off higher.

Compare that to the big decline that started in late 2022. Once ALB started to trade below its 30-week EMA and the black PPO line started reading less than zero, that was a clear indication to sell ALB and look for other opportunities.

Momentum is now both short- and long-term bullish. This is a situation that is similar to the bullish period that began in early 2020.

Volume is the third technical element I consider when evaluating a stock to buy. I want to own stocks that the institutional money managers are also buying. They only buy shares that they believe are undervalued.

The volume pane in Chart 1 shows red and black volume bars. The red volume bars show the number of shares traded for the week when the share price closed lower for the week. The black bars show the number of shares traded for the week when the price closed higher for the week. Bullish volume activity is when there is a higher frequency of black bars than red bars and when the black bars are higher than the red bars.

Since the bottom in April 2025, the volume picture has leaned bullish. As the price moved higher, there was a higher frequency of black bars, and they were generally higher than the red volume bars. It looks like ALB has some institutional money manager support.

Relative strength is the last technical element I consider when analyzing a stock. I want to buy stocks that are outperforming the SP 500 index. The relative strength line shown in the bottom pane of Chart 1 helps me do just that.

When the black relative strength line is rising, that means that ALB is outperforming the S&P 500 index. See the period from mid-2020 to the end of 2023. When the black relative strength line is falling, then ALB is underperforming the S&P 500 index. This can be seen from the end of 2023 to April 2025.

The most recent bullish price action since April 2025 has been in line with the advance of the S&P 500 index. The relative performance has been about even as I see it.

In summary, ALB sells a product that is expected to have very high demand moving forward. The price for this product is now stabilizing and should increase, leading to better earnings and revenues for ALB in 2026 and beyond. From a technical perspective, ALB has bullish price action and both short-term and long-term bullish momentum. The volume picture leans bullish, and the relative strength is in line with the SP 500 index since April 2025. I think that ALB is bullish, and I am considering buying shares in the next 72 hours.

data.title: Bally's: Deleveraging To Leverage Again
data.description:
Another quarter goes by, and Bally’s (NYSE:BALY) is still the underdog in the casino space.

The company keeps chasing an aggressive expansion, scooping up underperforming assets—like the deal for Australia’s Star Entertainment Group (OTCPK:EHGRF) and the earlier merger with Queen Casino & Entertainment—all while sitting on a mountain of debt.

Then something shifted. There was finally a glimpse of hope that Bally’s could chip away at its debt and maybe shift toward healthier growth by tightening up operations at these properties.

And of course, they needed to cover many gaps in this regard.

Star, for instance, was already drowning in issues—stacked-up net losses, constant dilution, and a battered reputation after AML probes. Queen Casino properties were also softer this year.

In my mind, the ideal scenario would be for Bally's to sell its iGaming business for cash only to Intralot (OTCPK:IRLTF), reduce its long-term debt by €2.7 billion (about $3.14 billion) to $450 million, and follow the playbook of regional property operations adjustments from top-tiers like Monarch Casino & Resorts (MCRI).

That would give them plenty of room to renovate, expand, and actually turn those properties into places customers want to visit.

After all, they would eventually report positive FCF and become a value play.

But things turned out a little differently.

Let me remind you:

  1. Bally's did indeed sell its iGaming division for €2.7 billion to Intralot, but with a deal that provided for €1.53 in cash and another €1.13 in Intralot shares (873.7 million shares that, adjusted for a significant risk premium due to its high debt, negative results, and low equity, would add ~$250 million to the Bally's shareholder);
  2. If we calculate current debt at $3.59 billion, we could say that Bally's would reduce it to approximately $1.82 billion (considering the exchange rate of 1 euro to 1.16 dollars). This would lead to Bally's trading from an EV of approximately $4.3 billion;
  3. Since iGaming represented approximately half of Bally's EBITDA, we could take half of what was reported and add it to Star's, which would come to something around $207.5 million;
  4. With a debt of $1.82 billion and this pro forma EBITDA, we would be dealing with a Debt/EBITDA ratio of 8.7x, which is very high. However, it is much lower than the 11x reported before the transaction;
  5. In this sense, we could also project an EV/EBITDA multiple of approximately 20.7x, which is extremely stretched for brick-and-mortar casino operators.

In my ideal scenario that I just told you about, with $450 million in debt on the balance sheet and $207.5 million in EBITDA, the stock would trade at a Debt/EBITDA of just 2.1x (which is pretty solid, if you ask me) and the stock would trade at an EV/EBITDA of 14.1x (expensive, but if they were to increase EBITDA they would soon squeeze and we could see an entry point in some cases).

Looking at Bally's long-term debt structure, we can make some pertinent observations.

About $480.5 million is locked in at a fixed 11% rate—well above market levels. What’s interesting is that this came right after the company had already issued debt at much cheaper levels, around 5.6% to 5.9%. That move pushes Bally’s cost of capital higher, especially since the rest of its debt is split between much lower fixed rates and floating rates, both well below that 11% mark.

In FY 2024, they spent $310.3 million on interest alone on the debt, growing 9.3% year-over-year.

With the $1.82 billion reduction and the repayment of those 11% notes, annual interest expenses should land around $100 million—over $200 million lower year-over-year.

That alone would get them to roughly break-even EBIT, maybe slightly negative. From there, the path forward is obvious: Tighten up operations, squeeze more margin out of the brick-and-mortar casinos, and bring SG&A down as a percentage of revenue.

What Lies Beyond?

By holding a majority stake in Intralot, Bally’s can still cash in on Bally’s International Interactive while maintaining a strategic foothold. That’s a big deal, since Intralot brings lottery know-how and a regulatory presence in nearly 40 countries.

So down the line, they could even sell it for a profit, and if that happens, part of it could go back to shareholders as a special dividend (just thinking out loud here).

But anyway, I still see two main risks in this deal (on top of what I already mentioned about the structure itself):

  1. There's still regulatory risk on the table, particularly exposing Bally's to greater European regulatory risks (which tend to be extremely burdensome in some cases). Of course, approval from the UK Gambling Commission is a great first step, but there's more to come;
  2. I really don't know if Intralot will be consolidated into Bally's financial statements. I don't think so, but if it does, we would likely see leverage headwinds on the balance sheet once again.

However, there are two other projects under development by Bally's that make me doubt whether their balance sheet will remain clean—or even close to it—for long.

The first is the two-phase construction of the Chicago casino.

Currently, Bally's is operating a temporary casino located at Medinah Temple (which has been in operation for at least two years), with Phase 2 expected to be completed by FY 2026. The entire project is expected to cost around $1.7 billion, backed by solid financing—including a $940 million construction loan from Gaming and Leisure Properties.

The permanent Grand Avenue casino will have 168,000 square feet of playable area, including 3,400 slot machines and 173 table games. F&B, public amenities, and a 500-room casino will also be included.

Another project worth mentioning is Bally's Bronx.

It's nothing more than a staggering $4 billion project planned for approximately 16 acres of underutilized parking lots and practice greens at the existing Bally's Golf Links site in Throggs Neck, The Bronx.

This project is much larger than the one in Chicago (a 500,000-square-foot gaming facility, a 500-room upscale hotel, flexible meeting spaces, and parking for 4,660 vehicles), which is expected to generate more than $1.5 billion in revenue for Bally's and more than $200 million in direct gaming taxes.

Currently, New York Mayor Eric Adams vetoed the City Council's decision to reject the rezoning proposal in late July (very recent developments), allowing the proposal to remain in contention for one of the casino licenses in southern New York State.

So you see, both are long-term drivers, but at the cost of one of the two:

  1. More long-term debt;
  2. Dilution of shareholders.

Honestly, I think it's interesting. But if Bally's can't manage its current assets sustainably, what makes anyone believe it'll suddenly become a top-tier operator just because it has bigger properties in the mix?

I don't think so. And there are probably more headwinds at this point (regulatory, financial, etc.) than tailwinds to keep the market happy.

Bally's Story in Q2

In Q2, Bally's reported what many like to call ‘mixed results,’ beating revenue estimates by at least $6.5 million but missing EPS by double digits.

Starting from the top, Bally's reported total revenue of $657.5 million, growing approximately 5.7% year-over-year. It's worth noting that this is the largest revenue growth since Q4 FY 2023 and interrupts a three-quarter decline.

But what drove this (besides the merger with Queen Casino & Entertainment early last year, of course)?

In my opinion, Bally's rode the wave of increased GGR from both brick-and-mortar casinos (+2.8% year-over-year) and iGaming (+32.2% year-over-year), causing Total GGR to grow 9.8% year-over-year to $19.4 billion in the United States.

And interestingly, the strongest growth came outside of Nevada.

That's why Nevada-centric operators like Golden Entertainment (GDEN) reported such a weak Q2, while other multi-state operators like Boyd Gaming (BYD) managed to maintain positive momentum.

For example, Bally's Atlantic City in New Jersey saw tailwinds, with GGR increasing 14.6% year-over-year in the quarter. Other states where Bally's has a presence and saw GGR increases were Colorado (+9%), Illinois (+16%), Delaware (+13.2%), among others.

Looking at slots, we saw 3.6% growth, while table games remained flat year-over-year.

Looking specifically at Bally's data, we have the following scenario for Q2 FY 2025:

  • Gaming: Revenue of $557.6 million in the quarter (+6.2%). Looking at the sub-segments, we saw revenue of $305.8 million (+19.7%) in Casino & Resorts, $195.8 million in International Interactive (-13.7%), and $55.9 million in North America Interactive (+33.1%). This is in line with AGA's data, with brick-and-mortar casinos increasing in the single digits and iGaming NA in the double digits;
  • Hotel: Revenue of $33.7 million (down 4.2% year-over-year). According to geolocation data, hotel traffic fell 1.6% year-over-year in April and June, growing only in May;
  • F&B: $34.8 million (+5.1%). This is one of the most surprising points to me, as most operators are failing to offset the decline in restaurant traffic through higher prices—something Bally's is demonstrating;
  • Licensing: Revenue of $7 million. Prior to Q2 FY 2025, revenue was negligible;
  • Retail, entertainment, and other: Total revenue of $24.3 million in the quarter (-14.7% year-over-year).

With SG&A of $274.4 million (41.7% of total revenue versus 37.3% in the same period last year), Bally's ended up reporting EBITDA of $93.1 million, a drop of nearly 11% year-over-year.

So, even with revenue growing in the mid-single digits year-over-year, margins are compressing—giving us room to rethink whether they're growing sustainably.

Looking a bit further down, Bally's reported EBIT of $21.3 million, a little over $6 million lower year-over-year. The quarterly EBIT margin was 3.2% (lower than the same period last year of 4.1% and lower than the 5-year average of 3.9%).

On the bottom line, they reported a net loss of $228.4 million, with interest on debt swallowing up all of the remaining profit.

Looking at cash flow, it's the same story again this quarter—operating cash flow isn't enough to cover the outflows from investing, so they've had to lean on more debt just to avoid draining their cash balance.

To be more precise, they reported an OCF of $16.8 million. This didn't even come close to offsetting the Capex ($49 million), much less the $142.8 million in negative cash flow from investing activities.

To offset the $126 million deficit, Bally's issued $110.1 million in net debt, helping them burn 'only' $23.8 million in cash.

They are currently sitting on $174.6 million in cash. The situation at this point is only sustainable because of the post-sale deleveraging of the iGaming business, allowing them to continue adding more debt and burning a few million in cash for a few years.

That's clearly not the ideal setup, especially if they're about to pile on even more debt to get Chicago off the ground, and maybe even the Bronx after that.

With at least another $1 billion needed just to build out the permanent casino on Grand Avenue and upwards of $4 billion for the over-the-top Bronx project, we're talking about several more billions in debt layered on top. And that’s before factoring in the extra borrowings they already lean on just to patch up their weak OCF.

The Future Is Cloudy for Bally's

In my last write-up, I changed my rating from 'Sell' to 'Hold' mainly because of deleveraging. But I'm becoming convinced that they will continue to borrow—perhaps more than before.

And it's interesting because since my first 'Sell' rating on the stock, it has fallen 11.8%, proving to be a very poor investment relative to the S&P, which rose over 31% in the same period.

And, as much as I hope that deleveraging could improve results in the short term, it's somewhat difficult to predict how much they will pump in the medium to long term with these massive projects underway.

Is it time to maintain a 'Hold' position or return to a 'Sell' position?

As I said before, with reduced debt, Bally's would be trading at an EV of $4.3 billion. With an EBITDA of $207.5 million (already consolidating Star's operations and removing iGaming), we'd be talking about a multiple of 20.7x!

In my previous perspective, they would focus on further debt reduction, but it doesn't seem like that will happen. Consider if they add at least another $1 billion in debt, we'd be dealing with an EV of $5.3 billion (and that's counting the debt added to cover the nonexistent FCF).

For Bally's to trade at a reasonable EV/EBITDA multiple (say, 12x), we'd need EBITDA to exceed $440 million, more than doubling in a year.

Trading at about 12x and no longer leveraging, I see fair value here at $7-8. Essentially, a 20-30% downside.

That said, and with concerns about leverage—including the upcoming leverage—I'm changing my rating to 'Sell' here.

I would change it to 'Hold' again if they decide to play it safe with the properties already in their portfolio, shifting FCF to positive and opting for sustainable growth.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Autodesk: AI Tailwinds Are Kicking In
data.description:
Since the start of 2025, one group of stocks has outperformed broadly in the stock market: large-cap tech names, particularly anything to do with AI. Investors have crowded into stocks like Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta (META), betting that it's the biggest leaders who stand most to benefit from the race to build out AI applications.

Amid this rush, there have been other beneficiaries as well. In Autodesk's (NASDAQ:ADSK) recent Q2 earnings report, the CAD design software company announced much higher-than-expected revenue growth thanks to clients rushing to build datacenters, which has helped Autodesk's billings in a time of otherwise weaker commercial activity. Autodesk stock jumped ~8% post-earnings, reversing prior losses and putting Autodesk into positive territory for the year.

I last wrote a neutral article on Autodesk in June, when the stock was trading modestly lower in the low $300s. I am incredibly impressed by the company's recent execution, as the strong revenue acceleration from AI data center use cases was unexpected. Autodesk is accelerating its billings at a time when many other enterprise software peers are struggling to maintain strong retention rates and land new business.

At the same time, however, in spite of the fact that Autodesk has traded in a range-bound fashion for most of the year and not seen substantial gains, I am concerned that the stock has become the classic tech play: high quality for a high price. I'm not convinced that positioning ourselves in a large-cap momentum tech stock like Autodesk is the way to go, when the S&P 500 is nervously treading water at all-time highs. I continue to prefer under-recognized and undervalued small and mid-cap tech stocks to crowded large-caps like Autodesk: so while I'm directionally more positive on the stock given the strength of its latest results, my valuation-focused investing priorities still put this stock at a neutral rating.

Since valuation is the crux of my skittishness on Autodesk, let's start with a discussion of where Autodesk sits against its latest outlook. At current share prices near $312, Autodesk trades at a market cap of $65.70 billion. After we net off the $2.52 billion of cash and marketable securities on Autodesk's latest balance sheet against $2.48 billion of debt, the company's resulting enterprise value is $65.66 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY26 (the year for Autodesk ending in January 2026), the company has lifted its full-year revenue growth expectations to $7.025-$7.075 billion, or 15% y/y growth, a two-point lift versus 13-14% previously. Recall that Autodesk is optically benefiting (several points of growth) this year from a change in its reseller contracts, which it will lap next year. The company is now also expecting 37% pro forma operating margins (versus 36-37% previously), which flows forward as $9.80-$9.98 in pro forma EPS, which is a 3% bump from its prior EPS expectations.

For the following year FY27, Wall Street analysts are expecting Autodesk to hit $7.81 billion in revenue (+11% y/y growth) and $11.22 in pro forma EPS (+13% y/y). This positions Autodesk's valuation multiples at:

  • 9.3x EV/FY26 revenue and 8.4x EV/FY27 revenue
  • 31.5x FY26 P/E and 27.8x FY27 P/E

It's difficult to digest these multiples and still believe that there's much further upside here, especially when we compare Autodesk against a raft of other large-cap enterprise software names.

Yes, there are examples like Microsoft (MSFT) and Oracle (ORCL) that trade at slightly richer valuation multiples. But on the whole, a number of other prominent software stocks which arguably also have AI tailwinds, like Salesforce (CRM) and Workday (WDAY), have been left behind with P/E multiples in the low to mid 20s. And in any case, the entire group is looking expensive (outside of out-of-favor stocks like Adobe (ADBE), where investors have deemed that generative AI will eventually rob Adobe of its creative suite customers) - so in my view, large-cap tech is not where you want to put your money anyway at this juncture.

All in all, I think Autodesk's strong performance prevents the stock from having any major downside, barring a major broad market correction: but at the same time, I don't see firm upside here. I prefer to remain on the sidelines until Autodesk corrects downward.

Autodesk Q2 results

Let's now go through Autodesk's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Autodesk's revenue grew 17% y/y to $1.76 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $1.73 billion (+15% y/y) by a two-point margin. Growth also accelerated two points versus 15% y/y growth in Q1.

The standout here was Autodesk's AECO (architecture, engineering and construction) segment, which was buoyed by demand for AI datacenters. On a constant currency basis, AECO revenue grew 24% y/y to $878 million, exactly 50% of the company's total revenue, which offset relatively slower growth in the company's media & entertainment and manufacturing product lines.

As a reminder, in the fourth quarter of last year Autodesk restructured its reseller agreements, emphasizing having more direct relationships with its customers. It also updated its accounting for reseller commissions, moving reseller expenses out of contra revenue and into sales and marketing expense (opex). This has the impact of boosting revenue (and shrinking operating margin percentage, while operating margin dollars remain consistent in both accounting scenarios).

As shown above, adjusted for the transaction model change, revenue growth would have been 11% y/y. We do find impressive, however, the fact that Autodesk's normalized billings growth clocked in at 26% y/y - indicating that revenue growth isn't likely to decelerate moving forward.

Nevertheless, we should be cognizant of the uncertain environment that Autodesk is operating in: in particular, management notes that it has a lot of renewals coming up in the fourth quarter. Per CFO Janesh Moorjani's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

The underlying momentum of the business in the second quarter of fiscal '26 was consistent with recent quarters and better than the more cautious assumptions built into the bottom end of our prior guidance range. The macroeconomic environment seems broadly stable, but uncertainty remains elevated. As usual, we have a large pool of EBA renewals to close in the back half of the year. We will also start cycling against tougher new transaction model billings and revenue growth comparisons with last year, particularly in the fourth quarter."

On the profitability front, Autodesk's pro forma operating margins rose one point y/y to 39% - in the absence of the transaction model change that boosted revenue, margins would have been two points higher at 41%. Autodesk's pro forma EPS of $2.62 also grew 22% y/y and beat Wall Street's expectations of $2.45 with 7% upside.

Key takeaways

High quality for a high price is not the style of investment I would favor in this market environment. Though Autodesk's growth acceleration is encouraging, it's unclear whether the company's current datacenter-driven billings spike will be transitory or sustained, and in any case, Autodesk's premium ~30x P/E multiples already more than compensate the stock for its strengths. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: IREN Q4 Earnings Review: Growth And Profit Matter
data.description:
Introduction

I've been covering IREN Limited (NASDAQ:IREN) since March this year, and even in that short time, it's up over 264%. It also was down by over 26% at some point between then and now, showcasing just how volatile this stock can be. Since its April lows, it has been on an almost straight line up.

What can you say? This market loves Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and AI companies.

Speaking of, IREN Ltd. is a mid-cap data center operator and Bitcoin miner. They are still in the early stages of building out operations, but they are hoping that they have positioned themselves in the right place at the right time to capitalize on both of these major trends.

On August 28th, they reported their Q4 FY25 earnings results, where they posted their first full-year profit. The stock jumped up over 25% before petering out a bit due to broader market woes. Like I said, this market is crazy for this kind of stuff, and IREN even defied the selloff in the tech sector for a while.

Let's get to their earnings, because that's the real reason for this update.

IREN Earnings

IREN led off with this image in its presentation, so I figured I would as well. It's a perfect encapsulation of why the market has rewarded this company so well over the last few quarters.

Like the title says: growth and profit matter in this market.

The flip into profitability territory was especially positive for the stock, which made up not only its YoY losses, but generated the first full-year profit on IREN's books. This is a seminal moment for tech stocks: becoming profitable.

Many never get there before they perish under the weight of their own cash burn to try to scale. IREN has been scaling very well, doubling its operating data center power capacity, raising its Bitcoin mining capacity by 400%, and doubling its GPU count YoY.

The profit came from a sudden burst of revenue this quarter. They made $87M for the year because they were able to make $187M this quarter. That is an insane catch-up for them, led by their Bitcoin operations.

IREN Bitcoin Operations

While IREN is both a data center operator (leasing to hyperscalers and other businesses needing capacity) and a Bitcoin miner, their most profitable operation by far is the Bitcoin mining.

While IREN was able to sell its Bitcoin for an average $99K over the quarter, they pay a little over a third of that in all-in costs. Their profit margins are driven by low power costs, equipment efficiency, and their good timing.

However, they have paused their Bitcoin mining expansion in favor of growing the AI business. They are still generating close to $1B in annual revenue from their 50EH/s of Bitcoin mining power, but we shouldn't expect that to grow too much until the AI side of the business is more robust.

Of course, the price of Bitcoin directly affects IREN's profitability for now, because much of their revenue comes from their ability to sell the Bitcoin they mine. It could be cut in half from its current price at over $100K before the revenue dries up to $0. That's some pretty solid margins, but also a risk to watch as Bitcoin is known to crash hard when it does, often more than 50%.

IREN AI & Data Centers

The AI Arms Race is still very much underway, and IREN is one of the buyers of GPUs. They have a current capacity for 60k GPUs, with a present build out of around 10k, and so have a long ways to go.

Here's the current counts:

IREN is positioning themselves along the Blackwell curve, hoping to cash in on the demand for these advanced chips.

On the data center side, current power capacity is 810MW, with a plan to expand to 2910MW in two years, a massive expansion across both British Columbia and Texas, where they have operations. The largest data centers included in these calculators are still under development, in Sweetwater, TX.

One of the nice things about IREN that investors value is that they own all of their own data centers, meaning that they are vertically integrated in a way that other cloud providers who just own the GPU clusters aren't, giving them a potential advantage in efficiency — something we've already seen in the Bitcoin side of things.

They also were able to secure the Nvidia (NVDA) "preferred partner" status, essentially a deal with NVDA to get good treatment when it comes to future GPU sales and generation rollouts. That's a big deal in the AI world, as it's a green-light for hyperscalers to work with you.

IREN Risks

Of course, mid-cap tech companies operating in this space are going to be risky. They have very high betas, and so IREN tends to jump faster than the market on good days but fall further on bad days. Risks to portfolios remain high — if IREN fell 70%, it would just be another day in the market. That would likely be alongside a Bitcoin crash or the AI bubble finally popping.

That being said, the best way to deal with these risks is to position yourself strategically in the stock. Make sure your allocation is prudent, so that if they are unable to build out the data centers they said they are, secure the right GPUs in enough quantity, or if their Bitcoin mining loses profitability, your portfolio won't break.

This is doubly true after the recent run-up, where IREN is now more highly valued than it has ever been. Its P/E has run up, and while it is still reasonable for a company of its size in its industry, it's significantly higher than the market has believed it should be for the last year, over three times as high from a forward P/E ratio standpoint.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I am going to continue holding onto my own position in IREN and will continue to issue a Buy rating. While I am not adding here, it is because I am content with my own position size, given the risks IREN introduces to my portfolio even at its small position size.

Investor position sizing remains one of the most important factors with stocks like IREN, as risks remain high despite the growth prospects and great news of profitability.

Things can unravel quickly, despite the upbeat presentation IREN gave. However, among companies in this space, I still believe IREN is one of the best, and could deserve a place in an aggressive portfolio.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Valuation Concerns And Uncharted Waters
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Nvidia report a mixed bag (0:20) Valuation concerns in tech (2:30) NIO and other earnings coming next week (7:40) Jobs report in uncharted waters (9:15).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, Seeking Alpha's Director of news. Welcome back to another week.

Brian Stewart: Thanks. Great to be here.

RS: NVIDIA (NVDA), is that where we're starting? That's what everybody's talking about. I feel like we haven't stopped talking about it this week on the podcast. And every analyst I know has something to say. What are you looking at when it comes to NVIDIA and what it means for the tech sector and the market at large?

BS: I think a mixed bag for NVIDIA. Just taking the the report at face value, I think it's pretty strong. They beat expectations, both earnings and revenue. They had a pretty solid outlook.

I think the issue such as it is is just how far the stock has come in such a short period of time. I mean, it's been a couple of years, but still, if you look at the rate of growth for the stock over that period of time, it's pretty impressive.

Valuations are high. And so the question becomes for investors, whether or not it can keep up the earnings growth to justify its current valuations. And really, I'm sure you've seen this in the people you've talked to as well.

There's really kind of a split decision on these earnings. There's some signs of slowing growth. So for instance, the data center revenue, data center is the main AI hub within the company. Data center revenue up 5% quarter over quarter, up 56% year over year, which obviously will be pretty high for almost any other company, but 56% is down from, I think, around the 70% range for the previous quarter.

So you're seeing a steady deceleration of that growth. So there's worry that NVIDIA's growth prospects are kinda capped. It relies on large US corporations as its main buyers.

Once they've eaten their fill in terms of AI infrastructure, NVIDIA might find trouble finding new places to grow, especially with the situation in China is unclear when the company will have an opportunity to see more growth there. And so with those kind of red flags on the horizon, investors are kinda taking a wait and see approach.

RS: And then broadly speaking, we have more news from some tech players. How would you feed that into this conversation, or what did you see out of the tech players outside of NVIDIA?

BS: The spectrum of big name earnings reports in the past week have been pretty narrow. So like you're saying, tech's been the main headline generator so far.

And there's a lot of companies that I think would be more under the radar in some other weeks, but because NVIDIA is just the only real headline player this week, you have some of them coming out.

So Snowflake (SNOW) is an example. It's a cloud based data platform provider. It's jumping as we're speaking now 19% after its earnings report. It's a 32% product revenue growth, thanks at least in part in leveraging AI adoption.

Like we've already talked about with NVIDIA, and this is gonna become a common theme for all the tech names that we're gonna mention. Valuation is a worry.

Another big name that we saw come out recently was CrowdStrike (CRWD) cybersecurity company. It initially dipped after its report was released in post market trading last night, but as we're sitting now recording on Thursday, it's up 3% on its earnings.

It beat expectations on earnings and revenue. The revenue forecast was a little soft, but that's what sent it lower after the market. But, analysts have reassessed that a little bit in the more medium term wake of its earnings report.

And so it's up now based on pretty strong ARR figure. So that's another mixed bag like we're talking about with NVIDIA. I think people are having a hard time figuring out how to take it.

I think there's an idea that execution both at NVIDIA and CrowdStrike is pretty strong, but given the valuation situation, there's sort of a sign of what's coming next.

And then the other tech name that we we saw this week, MongoDB (MDB), up 38% after its earnings announcement. It beat expectations, raised guidance. It's getting a lot of applause for implementing cost controls. It added a new CFO in April taking a a victory lap with with this first earnings report after that.

It's also benefiting from AI adoption, so no surprise there that there's a common theme among these companies, touting either in NVIDIA's case providing the infrastructure for other companies' AI and then for some of the other ones we've mentioned, they're using that AI to improve their product.

So if you're looking long term, so far, this week has been relatively positive in terms of tech continuing to be the engine for this market.

The only worry, especially as underlined by NVIDIA, is the valuation concerns, whether or not we outrun this first wave of growth and whether there's a correction coming in the future.

RS: I talked to Gary Vaughan this week who, if you want a good time and good insight, listen to that episode and anything that Gary has to say.

But the title of that episode is More volatility ahead in this AI bull market, which I think speaks to your point of this bullishness afoot, specifically when it relates to AI and tech, but there's not necessarily that bullishness as you look across the market.

BS: I haven't done a study on this, but just anecdotally looking through the headlines of the analysts on Seeking Alpha, I see a lot more red flags to look for volatility ahead.

Here's the problem with the market now. AI bubble starting to deflate. Those kind of headlines, I think, are becoming more prominent.

As an investor, obviously, you have to weigh, okay, is that a sign of things to come or is that the wall of worry that the market's gonna continue to climb?

Eventually, it's always gonna come down to the fundamentals, right? Like whether or not these companies can execute.

RS: But, also, is it just gonna come down to the fundamentals? That's the thing.

BS: Well, presumably, there's the the old saw that the market can stay rational longer than you can stay solvent.

RS: But, valuation seems a tough needle to thread these days.

BS: Yeah, but eventually, you run out of unsustained optimism. You know? Eventually you need something in the real world to justify.

RS: Just look at the cannabis sector.

BS: So I think if you're a tech investor and a market investor as a whole because tech has obviously been the main driver so far this year, I think that's going to be the constant question is where valuations are and whether or not there's going to be something that can kind of break.

If you believe it's an AI bubble, kind of prick that bubble. And even if you don't, take some of the air out of the market. And it could be unexpected events, a geopolitical event, something of that nature that just sort of disrupts the way things are going.

So it's just a matter of managing those long term expectations with the medium term, I think.

RS: So next week, what do we have earnings wise to look forward to?

BS: No real big headline there, but a couple of interesting more sector plays. So Nio (NIO), which is a Chinese EV maker, they're up 45% so far this year, 27% in the past month.

They got a big boost earlier this month when they announced a plan to expand their footprint. So they're expanding their global distribution.

I think the main debate going into this earnings report is the margins for their cars versus their pretty lofty expansion plan. With the aggressive expansion comes new execution risks. Whether or not the company can fulfill expectations there.

This earnings report might be a little early to figure that out because they were just announcing that expansion, but certainly look for commentary about what's next.

And then there's no profit in sight. It's been losses every quarter. Any update on when they might be able to see profits would be, I'm sure, well received by investors.

And then the other major earnings next week is Salesforce (CRM). They're down 24% year to date. One of the outliers to the downside among the major technology plays.

People see them as having a strong market position and they should benefit from AI driven growth, but it hasn't happened yet. Their sales growth has decelerated so far, which explains why the stock price has languished a bit.

So look for, if you're an investor, look for signs that they're starting to get some traction, starting to have some of the AI innovations that they've been touting drop to the bottom line.

RS: And macro wise, Cook was cooked this week. We saw a fired Fed governor. That seems to have been the main macro news.

Anything to say there or and or anything else to point to?

BS: We're in uncharted waters, in terms of whether or not she's actually fired. There's also a Fed meeting on the horizon that's coming up in mid September.

So whether or not she sits for that, whether or not she can participate in that process is going to be, I'm sure, part of the legal fights on the subject of Trump firings.

Next week, we're also gonna see the jobs report famously or well documented, that Trump fired the head of the BLS after the last jobs report, which showed major revisions, major downward revisions for the previous couple months, was one of the worst jobs reports in a while.

Going to this jobs report, both looking to see whether or not the weakness in the labor market that we saw last time is gonna continue. But there's also hanging over it, general concerns about how much we can trust the data.

I'm sure if there's a snap back in the number of jobs, there's gonna be a lot of people saying, well, Trump fired the old BLS head and now there's a new one and he's giving Trump what he wants.

So there's an issue where you're starting to undermine the trust and the data coming out. We'll see how the market reacts, if it's good news or bad news.

Good good thing to note is that, markets are closed on Monday for Labor Day. Enjoy the long weekend!

data.title: Boeing + Spirit: Getting The Supply Chain Back Together
data.description:
Introduction

The Boeing Company’s (NYSE:BA) move to reacquire Spirit AeroSystems Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:SPR) is defying the company’s entire recovery effort. For two decades, Boeing relied on a heavily outsourced industrial system, assuming that cost savings and risk-sharing agreements would outweigh the downsides. That model broke down when aircraft programs became more complex, and suppliers like Spirit happened to be under financial stress they could not absorb. The result is a fragile chain that seemed not to be working at the most important moments.

Bringing Spirit back under Boeing’s control (after Boeing spun it off in 2005) is about tightening the company’s grip on the part of the system most exposed to scrutiny and most critical to production.

Besides, Boeing begins to stabilize now with increasing deliveries and more orders, so it’s not the worst time for the deal. The only thing that is standing between these two companies is the regulators. The UK has already cleared the transaction, and Boeing is seeking the EU’s approval, which should arrive by the end of September. According to the deal, Airbus will be taking over some parts of Spirit as well, as they have been working together for a long time. That makes the current moment a natural point to take a look at and try to understand what this transaction means for Boeing’s recovery and how investors should think about both BA and SPR shares.

Boeing Today

Boeing’s Q2 results showed the first real feel of momentum since 2018. The revenue is $22.7B, a much smaller core loss per share of -$1.24 vs. -$2.90 a year ago, and only a $200M free-cash outflow as deliveries improved.

On the production side, Boeing has stabilized the 737 MAX at 38 aircraft a month and the 787 at seven a month. Management keeps emphasizing that the focus is on holding those levels steady before pushing higher. After five years of setbacks and scrutiny, that approach feels less like a message to regulators and more like the only sensible way to run the business. Boeing’s backlog sits at ~$522B with more than 5,900 aircraft, effectively years forward if they execute.

Defense made big progress as well, from a double-digit negative margin last year to a small positive (1.7%) this year, while Services have a ~20% margin and healthy order flow. The balance sheet still carries heavy debt, but cash plus securities of ~$23B and an incremental debt tick-down show they’re managing liquidity while they stabilize output. So, Boeing is still in losses, but the execution is finally moving the right way.

Spirit Today

Spirit had many problems over the past years, among those were constant cost overruns, cash strain, and the publicity for Boeing's huge quality issues. The original outsourcing idea back in 2005 (splitting off Spirit so it could attract work from multiple manufacturers) no longer fits today’s industry. It’s just another reminder of how quickly everything changes nowadays.

The situation is further complicated by Spirit’s mix of partners. Alongside Boeing, it also supplies Airbus SE (OTCPK:EADSF) and other programs, which creates a conflict once Boeing takes control because Airbus is a main competitor of theirs. So, the structure of this deal is like this: Boeing will take back the core Boeing work, including the 737 fuselage, while Airbus takes over its European programs that have been losing money. Anything that doesn’t fit with either side is being sold off or shut down. To make the transition easier, Spirit has already started selling non-core facilities, such as the site in Subang, Malaysia.

Why Boeing Needs To Own Spirit Again

  • The strongest argument for this deal is quality. Boeing has spent the better part of a decade under a microscope, and every slip with production turns into a major deal and is heavily discussed in the media. By owning Spirit again, Boeing cuts out a layer of distance between the engineers who design the airplanes and the teams who build the fuselage. If something goes wrong, Boeing’s own managers and inspectors can walk into the facility and address it directly, rather than negotiating fixes with a separate company. That speeds up problem-solving, lowers the risk of rework piling up, and avoids the kind of public drama that has followed the 737 program.

  • The second reason is supply chain resilience. The idea of outsourcing to lighten the balance sheet once looked attractive, but it also concentrated risk in a single supplier. Re-integrating Spirit gives Boeing more control over its production and greater leverage with smaller suppliers further down the chain.

  • There is also the regulatory dimension. The FAA and overseas regulators want to see a production system where processes are documented, consistent, and enforced under one roof for safety.

  • Financially, this deal looks less attractive. Spirit comes with losses and operational baggage, and Boeing will have to spend time and money cleaning that up, while Boeing itself is not even profitable yet.

The Stocks

Boeing

Boeing is still in the middle of a recovery. The second quarter results show progress, but profitability has not yet returned. Management says they are in no rush to chase higher output until the production rates are stable.

(Click on image for better quality).

The integration of Spirit changes the risk profile of the entire plan. If Boeing succeeds, quality problems at the fuselage stage should decline, the amount of rework should fall, and deliveries should move through the system with fewer delays. That, in turn, makes Boeing’s path to sustained free cash flow easier to believe. Demand is definitely there with Ryanair, Korean Air, and others committing to large orders, and the $522 billion backlog is proof of that. But the execution risk remains intact.

Integrating Spirit could take longer and cost more than expected, and any additional delays in certifying new aircraft models could pull management’s attention away from the recovery. I would very much hate for them to put out a million small fires due to the acquisition digestion. For long-term investors, though, this is exactly the type of moment when positioning early can pay off before the improvements are fully reflected in the stock.

Spirit AeroSystems

Spirit’s stock now trades almost entirely as a derivative of Boeing’s share price and the mechanics of the deal. With Boeing in the mid-$230s, the lower end of the exchange ratio values Spirit shares only a few percent higher than where they currently trade. That small gap reflects the limited arbitrage opportunity. This whole story is now about holding through the transaction while the spread between the trading price and the implied Boeing exchange value gradually closes.

The main risk for Spirit holders is that if Boeing’s stock weakens significantly, the implied value of the deal falls with it. There is little independent catalyst for Spirit at this stage because its future is effectively tied to Boeing’s performance and the successful closing of the acquisition. For that reason, the most rational stance is to view Spirit as a Hold rather than a buy, waiting for the final approvals and eventual closing.

Conclusion

If you’re trying to decide whether this is a trade or an investment, here’s how I see it.

Spirit is now a closing-risk, spread-capture story. Hold makes sense in EU approval.

Boeing is the real question each one must answer for themselves. The numbers are not at all pretty; they are at a negative operating profit. In many cases, it means this business is not worth keeping. But at the same time, it’s a huge legacy player, and the direction finally looks right. Reabsorbing Spirit is the uncomfortable work they have to do, but it brings a whole bunch of risks investors should be aware of.
